question_title: Will CDC’s official U.S. human A(H5N1) case tally increase by at least one before April 30, 2026?
summary: The CDC's official US human A(H5) case tally stands at 71 as of March 16, 2026. The last case was confirmed in November 2025 (an H5N5 case in Washington state), and there have been no new cases in ~4 months. During the current "slow regime" since February 2025, only 1 case has occurred in 13 months (rate ~0.077/month). The Poisson base rate model gives P(≥1 in 45 days) = 11%. However, massive ongoing poultry outbreaks (PA 7.6M birds, WI 1M+, CO, OH) and spring migration season create elevated exposure risk. A regime-switching Monte Carlo model accounting for the possibility of a new case cluster gives ~28%. I estimate 25%, weighted toward the slow-period rate but with meaningful uplift for the scale of current poultry outbreaks and the low threshold of only needing one case.

factors.description: Very low recent case rate: only 1 US H5 case in 13 months (Feb 2025 - March 2026), base rate ~0.077 cases/month during current regime
Massive ongoing poultry outbreaks in early 2026 (PA 7.6M birds, WI 1M+, CO, OH) creating significant worker exposure risk
Spring migration season (March-May) increases wild bird H5N1 circulation and spillover risk
CDC surveillance through Jan 2026 shows no unusual influenza activity; 130+ people tested with no positives in recent period
Only 1 case needed to resolve YES - very low threshold
Short 45-day window limits opportunity for case to occur AND be confirmed by CDC
Dairy cow outbreak (main driver of 2024 peak) appears largely contained since mid-2025

reasoning:
**Forecast: 25% probability (YES)**

**Key reasoning:**

- **Current CDC tally**: 71 confirmed H5 human cases as of March 16, 2026 (baseline). Needs to reach 72+ by April 30.
- **Case drought**: Only 1 case in the past 13 months (November 2025). The pure base-rate model gives just ~11% probability of a new case in 45 days.
- **But exposure risk is elevated**: Massive poultry outbreaks in early 2026 — Pennsylvania (7.6M birds), Wisconsin (1M+), Colorado, Ohio — create ongoing worker exposure. Spring migration increases wild bird H5N1 circulation.
- **Dairy containment**: The 2024 dairy cow outbreak that drove most cases appears contained. No dairy-linked cases since mid-2025.
- **Surveillance finding nothing**: CDC tested 130+ people between Sep 2025 - Feb 2026 with no positives, suggesting the case drought is real, not just surveillance gaps.
- **Regime-switching model**: Accounting for tail risk of a new case cluster gives ~28%. Final estimate of 25% reflects this uncertainty while anchoring on the observed low rate.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will CDC’s official U.S. human A(H5N1) case tally increase by at least one before April 30, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42810)
[Global Summary of Recent Human Cases of H5N1 Bird Flu](https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/spotlights/h5n1-summary-08042025.html)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if CDC’s official U.S. human A(H5N1) case tally is at least one higher on April 30, 2026 than it was on March 16, 2026, using CDC’s A(H5) Bird Flu Current Situation page at [https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/situation-summary/index.html](https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/situation-summary/index.html) It resolves to no otherwise.
fine_print: Use CDC’s own confirmed U.S. human case count, not state reports or media reports that CDC has not yet incorporated. If the summary page is unavailable, use CDC’s human-case surveillance chart. Candidate Sources: CDC Global Human Cases with Influenza A(H5N1) page [https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/php/surveillance/chart-epi-curve-ah5n1.html](https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/php/surveillance/chart-epi-curve-ah5n1.html)
